title: Atlas VPN Review 2026: Discontinued — Service Shut Down in April 2024

reviewContent:
## Atlas VPN Is Discontinued

**Atlas VPN permanently shut down on April 24, 2024.** You can no longer sign up, download the apps, or connect to any Atlas VPN servers. The service is gone.

If you are reading this because you searched for an Atlas VPN review, the short answer is: do not look for ways to download or use Atlas VPN. It does not exist anymore. This page explains what happened, what it means for former users, and which VPN alternatives you should consider instead.

## What Happened to Atlas VPN?

Atlas VPN was founded in 2019 as a freemium VPN service based in Peakstar Technologies Inc., registered in Delaware, USA. It gained traction quickly by offering something rare in the VPN market: a genuinely usable free plan with 5 GB of monthly data, no ads, and access to servers in three countries.

In October 2021, Nord Security — the parent company behind NordVPN and Surfshark — acquired Atlas VPN. At the time, Atlas VPN had roughly 6 million users worldwide. Nord Security stated that Atlas VPN would continue operating independently and pursuing its freemium strategy.

That independence lasted about two and a half years.

On March 25, 2024, Atlas VPN announced it would cease operations effective April 24, 2024. The official statement cited "insurmountable challenges" in a highly competitive market, along with rising operational costs. In plainer language: Nord Security decided it was not worth running three separate VPN brands and consolidated Atlas VPN's paying customers into NordVPN.

## What Happened to Atlas VPN Users?

All paid Atlas VPN subscribers were automatically migrated to NordVPN. They received full access to NordVPN's premium features for the remainder of their existing Atlas VPN subscription period, at no additional cost. This was a significant upgrade — NordVPN offers over 7,000 servers in 118 countries, six independently audited no-logs reports, and a far more mature feature set.

Free Atlas VPN users were not so lucky. Free accounts were simply terminated on the shutdown date. There was no migration path, no compensation, and no alternative offered. If you were using Atlas VPN's free tier, you were left to find a replacement on your own.

## How Good Was Atlas VPN When It Was Active?

Atlas VPN was a decent budget VPN with some genuine strengths and some serious weaknesses.

### The Good

**Speed was genuinely impressive.** Atlas VPN used the WireGuard protocol and delivered speed retention of around 88-94% on nearby servers. Multiple independent tests recorded download speeds above 250 Mbps on a 300 Mbps connection. For a budget VPN with a free tier, this was well above average.

**The free plan was one of the best available.** While limited to 5 GB per month and four server locations (Amsterdam, Los Angeles, New York, and Singapore), it required no payment information, showed no ads, and supported P2P traffic. In a market where most "free VPNs" are data-harvesting operations, Atlas VPN's free tier was legitimate.

**Apps were clean and simple.** The interface was minimalist and beginner-friendly across Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS. Setup took under two minutes.

### The Bad

**A critical zero-day vulnerability in September 2023 exposed Linux users' real IP addresses.** A security researcher publicly disclosed that Atlas VPN's Linux client had an unauthenticated API endpoint listening on localhost port 8076. Any website could exploit this to disconnect the VPN and reveal the user's actual IP address. While Atlas VPN patched the issue in version 1.1, the vulnerability was initially disclosed on Reddit rather than through responsible channels — suggesting the researcher had difficulty getting Atlas VPN's attention through normal reporting processes.

**No independent security audit was ever published.** Atlas VPN claimed a no-logs policy but never hired a third-party firm to verify it. Given its US jurisdiction and the 2023 security incident, this was a significant trust deficit.

**The server network was small.** Approximately 1,000 servers across 49 locations is a fraction of what NordVPN (7,000+), Surfshark (3,200+), or CyberGhost (11,000+) offer. This meant fewer options for bypassing geo-restrictions and more congestion during peak hours.

**US jurisdiction under the Five Eyes alliance** meant that Atlas VPN could theoretically be compelled to provide user data via FISA orders or National Security Letters — though without logs, there would theoretically be nothing to hand over.

## What Should You Use Instead?

If you were an Atlas VPN user — or are simply looking for a VPN in the same category — here are the best alternatives in 2026:

### Best Free Alternative: Proton VPN

Proton VPN offers a **genuinely unlimited free plan** with no data caps. Free users get access to servers in five countries, and the service is based in Switzerland with a strong privacy track record. It is the clear successor to what Atlas VPN's free tier offered, but better in every way.

### Best Budget Alternative: Surfshark

Surfshark starts at around **$2.19/month** on a two-year plan and offers unlimited simultaneous connections, 3,200+ servers in 100 countries, and independently audited no-logs policy verified by Deloitte. It fills the same budget niche Atlas VPN occupied, with far more features.

### Best Overall: NordVPN

If you were migrated from Atlas VPN to NordVPN, you already have the upgrade. NordVPN is our top-rated VPN with 7,000+ servers, six published no-logs audits, post-quantum encryption, and the fastest speeds we have tested. Former Atlas VPN users who landed on NordVPN got a genuinely better product.

## FAQ

**Is Atlas VPN still available?**
No. Atlas VPN permanently shut down on April 24, 2024. The website, apps, and all servers have been decommissioned. You cannot sign up for or use Atlas VPN.

**What happened to my Atlas VPN subscription?**
All paid Atlas VPN subscribers were automatically migrated to NordVPN with full premium access for the remainder of their subscription period. Free users' accounts were terminated with no migration option.

**Why did Atlas VPN shut down?**
Atlas VPN cited insurmountable competitive challenges and rising costs. The practical reason is that its parent company, Nord Security, decided to consolidate its VPN portfolio. Running three VPN brands (NordVPN, Surfshark, and Atlas VPN) was no longer justified.

**Was Atlas VPN safe to use?**
Atlas VPN had mixed security credentials. While it used industry-standard WireGuard encryption, a critical zero-day vulnerability in September 2023 leaked Linux users' IP addresses. The service never published an independent security audit, and it operated under US jurisdiction. For the time it was active, it was adequate for casual use but not recommended for high-stakes privacy.

**What is the best free VPN alternative to Atlas VPN?**
Proton VPN is the best free alternative. Unlike Atlas VPN's 5 GB monthly limit, Proton VPN's free plan has no data cap. It is based in Switzerland, has been independently audited, and offers servers in five countries at no cost.

**Can I still download Atlas VPN?**
No. The Atlas VPN apps have been removed from all app stores and the official website no longer offers downloads. Any third-party sites claiming to offer Atlas VPN downloads should be avoided — they are likely distributing modified or malicious software.
